coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of carbon quantum dot ‑carbon nanotube composites on an eggshell-derived catalyst by one-step chemical vapor deposition
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hong
Chen, Qiang
Tang, Xiaodong
Peng, Xinyue
Deng, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) based composites such as carbon quantum dot-CNT (CQD-CNT) demonstrate excellent performance in the fields of bioimaging and biomedicine. Nevertheless, CQD-CNT composites were mostly prepared by multiple and cumbersome steps, including the synthesis of CQDs, functionalization of CNTs and subsequent assembly of CQDs on CNTs. Here, a facile one-step synthesis of CQD-CNT was successfully conducted on an active CaO supported FeCo (Fe-Co/CaO) catalyst derived from waste eggshells via chemical vapor deposition, and other CNT-based composites such as carbon fiber-graphene sheet-CNT (CNF-GNS-CNT) and CNF-CNT as well as CNTs were also synthesized by modulating the catalyst support, growth temperature and growth time. The catalysts and produced CNTs and CNT-based composites were analyzed by multiple characterization tools. The results showed that nano CaO supported FeCo catalyst only synthesized MWCNTs, whereas Fe-Co/CaO was favorable for the synthesis of CNT-based composites. Cobalt oxides and iron oxides were well dispersed in the fresh Fe-Co/CaO catalyst, and FeCo alloy particles of different sizes were formed after catalyst reduction and heating to different growth temperatures. At a low growth temperature of 750 °C, CNF-GNS-CNT was synthesized on Fe-Co/CaO, in which CNTs and CNFs were grown on small (~10 nm) and larger (~30 nm) metal particles, respectively, whereas GNSs were mainly produced on CaO. At a high growth temperature of 850 °C, surface area and pore volume of catalysts decreased greatly, and higher solubility of carbon atoms in metal particles resulted in less desorbed CHx, which is not favorable for the growth of GNSs. Instead, CQD-CNT composites were selectively synthesized at 850 °C, which was ascribed to the oxidation corrosion of CNFs by OH radicals, but with the extension of growth time, only CNTs were produced due to the complete oxidation of CQDs. Further, larger metal particles were produced at a higher temperature of 950 °C, which were wrapped by graphite, and the partial deactivation of metal particles led to a few small particles remained active for CNT growth with a low carbon yield.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Carbon nanotubes
One-step CVD
CNT-based composites
Growth temperature
Growth time

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Open Fund of Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Solid Wastes Pollution Control and Recycling ( 2020B121201003 ) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project 51602268 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD profiles of CaO and nano CaO; (b) XRD profiles of Fe-Co/CaO and Fe-Co/Nano CaO catalysts; XPS spectra of Fe-Co/CaO and Fe-Co/Nano CaO catalysts at (c) Co 2p and (d) Fe 2p regions.
SEM images and EDX mapping of Fe-Co/CaO (a,b) and Fe-Co/Nano CaO (c,d).
(a,b) SEM images; (c) Uv-vis and (d) Raman spectra of CNM-Nano-850 and CNM-850.
XRD profiles of CNMs grown on Fe-Co/CaO catalysts at different temperatures (a) and purified CNMs (b).
SEM and TEM images of CNMs synthesized on Fe-Co/CaO catalysts at 750 °C (a,d,g), 850 °C (b,e,h) and 950 °C (c,f,i).
(a)Uv-vis and (b) Raman spectra of CNMs grown on Fe-Co/CaO catalysts at different temperatures.
SEM and TEM images of CNM-850-10 (a, c) and CNM-850-60 (b,d).
Schematic illustrations of CNMs grown on Fe-Co/CaO catalysts at different grown temperatures and growth time.
The carbon yields of CNMs synthesized at different growth temperatures and the purity of CNMs after HCl treatment.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of carbon quantum dot ‑carbon nanotube composites on an eggshell-derived catalyst by one-step chemical vapor deposition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, No. 8 Xindu Avenue, Xindu District, Chengdu 610500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, No. 8 Xindu Avenue, Xindu District, Chengdu, China, 610500

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Solid Wastes Pollution Control and Recycling, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, Guangdong 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Solid Wastes Pollution Control and Recycling

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Solid Wastes Pollution Control and Recycling, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, Guangdong, China, 510006

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) based composites such as carbon quantum dot-CNT (CQD-CNT) demonstrate excellent performance in the fields of bioimaging and biomedicine. Nevertheless, CQD-CNT composites were mostly prepared by multiple and cumbersome steps, including the synthesis of CQDs, functionalization of CNTs and subsequent assembly of CQDs on CNTs. Here, a facile one-step synthesis of CQD-CNT was successfully conducted on an active CaO supported FeCo (Fe-Co/CaO) catalyst derived from waste eggshells via chemical vapor deposition, and other CNT-based composites such as carbon fiber-graphene sheet-CNT (CNF-GNS-CNT) and CNF-CNT as well as CNTs were also synthesized by modulating the catalyst support, growth temperature and growth time. The catalysts and produced CNTs and CNT-based composites were analyzed by multiple characterization tools. The results showed that nano CaO supported FeCo catalyst only synthesized MWCNTs, whereas Fe-Co/CaO was favorable for the synthesis of CNT-based composites. Cobalt oxides and iron oxides were well dispersed in the fresh Fe-Co/CaO catalyst, and FeCo alloy particles of different sizes were formed after catalyst reduction and heating to different growth temperatures. At a low growth temperature of 750 °C, CNF-GNS-CNT was synthesized on Fe-Co/CaO, in which CNTs and CNFs were grown on small (~10 nm) and larger (~30 nm) metal particles, respectively, whereas GNSs were mainly produced on CaO. At a high growth temperature of 850 °C, surface area and pore volume of catalysts decreased greatly, and higher solubility of carbon atoms in metal particles resulted in less desorbed CHx, which is not favorable for the growth of GNSs. Instead, CQD-CNT composites were selectively synthesized at 850 °C, which was ascribed to the oxidation corrosion of CNFs by OH radicals, but with the extension of growth time, only CNTs were produced due to the complete oxidation of CQDs. Further, larger metal particles were produced at a higher temperature of 950 °C, which were wrapped by graphite, and the partial deactivation of metal particles led to a few small particles remained active for CNT growth with a low carbon yield.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have been applied in the fields of optics, catalysis, electronics and energy due to their unique structures and physical, chemical, mechanical, and optical properties [1–6]. However, CNT's high hydrophobicity and cytotoxicity and insufficient functional groups limited their potential applications in the fields of bioimaging and biomedicine [7]. To further improve the physicochemical properties of CNTs, other carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) such as zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), one-dimensional carbon nanofibers (CNFs), and two-dimensional graphene nanosheets (GNSs), were added to incorporate with CNTs to form CNT-based composites [8–11]. Among those CNMs, CQDs display good water solubility due to the abundance oxygen-containing functional groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl) on their surface, thus the formation of CQD-CNT composites by immobilizing CQDs on the CNT surface can introduce sufficient functional groups on CNTs, which effectively altered CNT's hydrophilicity. Meanwhile, the low toxicity of CQDs can significantly decrease the cytotoxicity of CNTs [12].

Currently, CNT-based composites have been widely prepared based on the synthesis of CNTs and other CNMs and the subsequent assembly of CNTs and other CNMs into composites. Especially for the preparation of CQD-CNT composites, it involves multiple steps, such as the synthesis of CQDs, modification of CNTs, and the combination of CNTs with CQDs. For example, Mallakpour et al. [13] prepared CQDs using a hydrothermal method, and oxidized CNTs in a mixture of nitric acid and sulfuric acid, and then conjugated CNTs with CQDs to form CQD-CNT. Mandani et al. [8] also adopted the hydrothermal method to prepare CQDs and then physically mixed with CNTs to form CQD-CNT composites with low cytotoxicity. Chen et al. [14] prepared CQD-CNT using procedures including the synthesis and functionalization of CQDs, acid oxidation of CNTs, and then bonding CQDs on the surface of CNTs, which enhanced the photovoltaic conversion efficiency. Although these studies prepared CQD-CNT with improved performance, the multiple and cumbersome steps for CQD-CNT preparation increased the operation cost and material cost, which greatly limited the practical applications of CQD-CNT composites. Therefore, a facile one-step synthesis method for CQD-CNT composites is highly desirable.

Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) has become the most common method for synthesizing CNMs due to its low cost, easy control and mass production [15,16]. So far, CNMs such as CNTs [17], CNFs [18], graphene [19], GNSs [20], GQDs [21,22], and their composites (CNF-CNT, graphene-CNT et al.) [23,24] have already been synthesized by CVD method, but there are very few studies on the synthesis of CQDs via CVD. Catalysts play a vital role in the synthesis of CNMs, and various types of catalysts have been employed for the synthesis of CNMs, in which supported metal catalysts were used for the synthesis of CNTs and CNFs [25], and metal oxide catalysts were utilized to synthesize GNSs [20], while metal foils were employed for the growth of graphene or GQDs [19,26]. Thus, it is feasible and intriguing to synthesize CNT-based composites (e.g. CQD-CNT) via CVD by development of catalysts.

CaO is an ideal candidate to be used in the synthesis of CNT-based composites via CVD. First, CaO is cost-effective and easy available, which can be originated from the calcination of natural limestones or calcium-containing wastes, such as eggshells [27]. Second, CaO is very easy to be removed [28], which is favorable for the purification of CNT-based composites. Moreover, CaO has been employed as catalysts for the synthesis of graphene or GNSs by CVD. For example, Shi et al. [29] used CaO with a layered porous structure obtained by calcining shells as a catalyst and template, and synthesized graphene foams with high purity and porosity, ultra-low density and excellent flexibility. Chen et al. [30] synthesized GNSs on CaO by catalytic CVD of ethanol at 950 °C. In addition to being as catalysts, CaO has been used as a common catalyst support to load metal catalysts for the synthesis of CNTs [31]. Although no studies have synthesized CNT-based composites (e.g. CQD-CNT) by using CaO supported metal catalysts, considering that the size of metal catalyst, the metal content, the type of the support, growth temperature, growth time, and gas flow rate all would affect the synthesis of CNMs via CVD method [32–34], we envision that CaO supported metal catalysts can be used to synthesize CNT-based composites by adjusting the conditions in the CVD process.

Here, CaO supported FeCo catalysts were prepared from waste eggshells, and employed to synthesize CNT-based composites by one-step CVD. Various characterization methods were used to characterize catalysts and CNMs grown at different conditions, including X-ray Diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) mapping, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), N2 physisorption, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), ultraviolet-visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–Vis), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and Raman spectroscopy. Unexpectedly, CQD-CNT was successfully synthesized on Fe-Co/CaO catalysts via one-step CVD, and other types of CNMs such as CNF-GNS-CNT, CNF-CNT and CNTs were controllably synthesized by modulating catalyst support, growth temperature and growth time. Based on the characterization results, we proposed a mechanism to explain the growth of CNT-based composites on the Fe-Co/CaO catalyst.

Eggshells (collected from our school canteens) were washed with deionized water, dried and calcined at 900 °C for 4 h in a muffled furnace. After calcination, the obtained CaO was ground into powder, and stored at room temperature in a glass vial sealed with the paraffin film. The Fe-Co/CaO catalyst with a total metal loading of 5 wt% was prepared by incipient wetness impregnation method [35,36]. The Fe/Co molar ratio in the catalyst was 1. Cobalt(II) acetylacetone (Co(acac)2, Aladdin, 97%) and iron(III) acetylacetone (Fe(acac)3, Energy Chemical, 99%) dissolved in anhydrous ethanol (Chengdu CHBON, ≥99.7%) were added to CaO powder. The mixture was stirred at 25 °C for 30 min, and then stirred at 60 °C until the formation of a viscous mixture. Subsequently, the mixture was dried at 80 °C for 12 h. Finally, the dried catalyst was ground and calcined at 450 °C for 3 h. We also used the same method to prepare nano CaO supported FeCo catalysts (Fe-Co/Nano CaO). Nano CaO support was obtained by Calcination-Hydration-Dehydration (C-H-D) method from eggshells [37]. First, eggshells were converted to CaO by calcining at 900 °C for 4 h, and then the obtained CaO was refluxed in water at 60 °C for 6 h to form Ca(OH)2. Finally, Ca(OH)2 was calcined at 800 °C for 3 h and decomposed into nano CaO.

CNMs (CNTs and CNT-based composites) were synthesized by atmospheric pressure CVD. First, the catalyst (150 mg) was loaded in a ceramic boat at the center of a tube furnace. The catalyst was then reduced under flowing H2 (1 bar, 30 sccm) from room temperature to 700 °C at 10 °C/min. When the temperature reached at 700 °C, the reactor was purged with Ar, while its temperature was further raised to a designated growth temperature (750 °C, 850 °C, and 950 °C, respectively). Next, the ethanol vapor was then introduced into the reactor by flowing Ar (500 sccm) for different time (10 min, 30 min, and 60 min, respectively). Afterwards, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature in Ar. The synthesis procedures via CVD are shown in Fig. S1. In order to obtain purified CNMs, the synthesized CNMs were soaked in dilute hydrochloric acid (Chengdu CHBON, 0.1 mol/L) for 2 h to remove the catalyst. The carbon yield of CNMs was calculated according to the following formula:
The carbon yield
wt %
=
M tot
−
M cat
/
M cat
× 100 %

Among them, Mtot is the total mass of the catalyst after growth, and Mcat is the mass of the catalyst after the same temperature program is carried out without ethanol. The Mtot and Mcat were both obtained by conducting three synthesis experiments to obtain their average values.

The catalysts, as-synthesized CNMs and purified CNMs were characterized by XRD. XRD was carried out on a diffractometer (Rigaku Smartlab, Japan) from 5 to 80° with the 2θ step size of 0.02° and the counting time of 12 s, using Cu Kα radiation at 40 kV and 40 mA. XPS spectra were collected with a monochromatized Al-Ka radiation source (Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi XPS System). Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of catalysts were measured using a pore size analyzer (Quantachrome, Quadrasorb SI) at 77 K. Before measurement, the sample was degassed at 250 °C for 10 h under high vacuum (<0.01 mbar). The specific surface areas (SSA) were calculated by the Brunauer, Emmet, and Teller (BET) method, while the total pore volume was calculated from the maximum adsorption of N2 at a relative pressure (P/P0) of 0.99. The morphology of catalysts and purified CNMs was observed by FESEM (GeminiSEM 300, ZEISS, Germany). TEM images of purified CNMs were obtained by a high resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI TECNAI G2F20, USA). The samples were dispersed in ethanol by bath sonication and then dropped onto an ultra-thin carbon film for TEM analysis. UV–vis absorption spectra of purified CNMs were obtained using a Shimadzu 1800 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The samples were dispersed in deionized water by bath sonication and then the supernatant was extracted by high-speed centrifugation (13,500 r/min) for analysis. TGA was used to characterize purified CNMs. TGA was conducted on a TG instrument (NETZSCH, STA 449 F3 Jupiter). For a typical test, about 10 mg purified CNMs were placed in an alumina pan, heated to 200 °C, and held for 10 min under airflow (50 sccm) to remove moisture. Subsequently, the temperature was continuously raised from 200 to 1000 °C at a 10 °C/min rate. The weight loss was monitored and recorded as a function of the temperature. As-synthesized CNMs were also characterized by Raman spectroscopy (ID Raman micro IM-52) in the backscattering configuration using a 785 nm laser.

XRD was employed to characterize the prepared CaO supports, as shown in Fig. 1a. The XRD profiles of CaO and Nano CaO both showed five diffraction peaks at 32.2°, 37.4°, 53.9°, 64.2° and 67.4°, corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) planes of CaO (PDF#48–1467), no other diffraction peaks were observed, which indicated that the calcination of eggshells mainly produced CaO, which is consistent with previous reports [27]. The Scherer equation was used to calculate the grain size of CaO and Nano CaO, which showed that CaO prepared by calcination at 900 °C has a larger size (>100 nm), while Nano CaO prepared by the C-H-D method has a much smaller size of 58.9 nm. The XRD patterns of Fe-Co/CaO and Fe-Co/Nano CaO catalysts were shown in Fig. 1b. The diffraction peaks at 23.1°, 29.4°, 36.0°, 39.4°, 43.2°, 47.5°, 48.5°, 57.4°, and 64.6° are ascribed to CaCO3, and the diffraction peaks at 18.0°, 34.1°, 50.8°, and 62.4° correspond to Ca(OH)2, whereas the intensity of diffraction peaks from CaO decreased significantly. This is due to the fact that CaO is easy to react with moisture to form Ca(OH)2 during the impregnation. Ca(OH)2 further reacted with CO2 generated by the decomposition of acetylacetonate from the metal precursors in the subsequent catalyst calcination, which produced CaCO3. In addition, Fe oxides and Co oxides are usually formed by the decomposition of Fe(acac)3 and Co(acac)2 during the catalyst calcination at 450 °C. However, the diffraction peaks of Fe oxide and Co oxide were not observed on both Fe-Co/CaO and Fe-Co/Nano CaO catalysts, which suggested that the metal species may be well dispersed on the supports [38].

XPS was further conducted to examine the metal species on Fe-Co/CaO and Fe-Co/Nano CaO catalysts. As shown in Fig. 1c and d, both catalysts exhibit similar XPS spectra. The XPS spectra of Co 2p in Fig. 1c have two major peaks with binding energies at 779.9 and 794.9 eV, corresponding to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively [39]. The binding energies and a spin energy separation of 15.0 eV indicate the presence of both Co2+ and Co3+, and two other peaks at 788.6 and 803.1 eV can be attributed to the satellite peaks of Co 2p [39]. These results confirmed the formation of Co3O4 nanoparticles on both catalysts. The XPS spectra of Fe 2p in Fig. 1d exhibit two characteristic peaks located at 711.0 and 724.4eV corresponding to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 spin orbitals of Fe2O3 [40], together with two satellite peaks at 717.7 and 732.6 eV. Combined XRD and XPS results, it can be concluded that cobalt oxides (CoxOy) and iron oxides (FexOy) nanoparticles were well dispersed on both catalysts.

The N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms of Fe-Co/CaO and Fe-Co/Nano CaO are shown in Fig. S2. Both catalysts show hysteresis loops belonging to the type IV isotherm, suggesting the existence of mesoporous structures in the catalysts. The specific surface area and pore volume of the Fe-Co/CaO catalyst are 8.45 m2/g and 0.035 cm3/g. In contrast, the Fe-Co/Nano CaO catalyst shows a slightly larger specific surface area and pore volume, which are 13 m2/g and 0.072 cm3/g respectively. We further characterized the morphology of catalysts by SEM, as shown in Fig. 2a and c. Both Fe-Co/CaO and Fe-Co/Nano CaO catalysts have layered flake structures. The size of individual flakes is in the range of 500 nm-1 μm, and flake aggregates of several micrometers were also observed in Fe-Co/CaO. In contrast, most particles in Fe-Co/Nano CaO have a nano size of 50–400 nm, which also aggregate into larger flakes up to several micrometers. The dispersion of metal species on the catalysts was further investigated by EDX mapping. As shown in Fig. 2b and d, Fe-Co/CaO and Fe-Co/Nano CaO catalysts both have relatively good dispersibility of metal species, which are consistent with XRD results. Based on the above characterization results, we can conclude that the metal species (CoxOy and FexOy) are well dispersed on supports in both Fe-Co/Nano CaO and Fe-Co/CaO catalysts, which may serve as active catalysts for CNMs synthesis.

Fe-Co/CaO and Fe-Co/Nano CaO catalysts were first employed to synthesize CNMs under the same growth condition, i.e., the catalyst was reduced at 700 °C and CNMs were grown at growth temperature of 850 °C for 30 min, and the resulting samples were labeled as CNM-850 and CNM-Nano-850, respectively. The morphology of CNM-850 and CNM-Nano-850 was characterized by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 3a and b, CNM-Nano-850 consisted of CNTs with a diameter of 15–25 nm, and the surface of CNTs is smooth. In contrast, CNM-850 contained CNTs with a larger diameter of 15–45 nm, and the CNT surface was covered by carbon nanoparticles with a diameter less than 5 nm. These carbon nanoparticles conform to the characteristics of CQDs in size. As CQDs usually show obvious light absorption in the ultraviolet region, CNM-Nano-850 and CNM-850 were dispersed in deionized water by sonication, and the black dispersions were then centrifuged to obtain the supernatant for Uv-vis analysis. As shown in Fig. 3c, CNM-850 has obvious absorption peaks around 220 nm and 255 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* transition of CC [41], confirming the formation of CQDs in the sample, while CNM-Nano-850 has no obvious absorption peaks. Raman spectroscopy was used to characterize the quality and degree of graphitization of CNMs. As shown in Fig. 3d, both spectra exhibit D band and G band, in which D band around 1300 cm−1 is attributed to the amorphous carbon and defects of graphite layers in CNMs, and the G band at ~1580 cm−1 is attributed to a splitting of the E2g stretching mode of the graphitic carbon [42]. The degree of graphitization and the number of defects can be evaluated by the area ratio between D band and G band (D/G). CNM-850 has a high D/G ratio of 3.17, indicating that it has many defects, which is attributed to the generation of CQDs with numerous surface defects. CNM-Nano-850 shows a much lower D/G ratio of 0.77, indicating that the sample contained CNTs with a higher degree of graphitization and less defects. Based on SEM, Uv-vis and Raman results, it can be concluded that both Fe-Co/CaO and Fe-Co/Nano CaO catalysts were active in the synthesis of CNMs, but Fe-Co/CaO synthesized CQD-CNT composites, and Fe-Co/Nano CaO produced CNTs. Considering the easy preparation of CaO and the advanced performance of CQD-CNT composites in the fields of photoelectronics, bioimaging, and biomedicine, we selected Fe-Co/CaO as the catalyst to synthesize CQD-CNT in subsequent studies.

Previous studies have shown that different growth temperatures can affect the types of synthesized CNMs [43,44]. Thus, we investigated the effect of growth temperatures on the synthesis of CQD-CNT from the Fe-Co/CaO catalyst. Except for the growth temperature of 850 °C, 750 and 950 °C were also adopted to synthesize CNMs, and the as-synthesized samples were labeled as CNM-750 and CNM-950. We first analyzed the carbon yields of the three CNM samples. Due to the high content of metal particles in as-synthesized CNMs, an overlap occurred between the oxidative weight loss of CNMs and the oxidative weight gain of metal particles in the TGA profile (as shown in Fig. S3), which makes it difficult to determine carbon yields through TGA. Thus, the carbon yields were calculated based on the formula as described in Section 2.2. Three synthesis experiments for each growth temperature under identical conditions were carried out to obtain the average yield. As shown in Table 1 , the average carbon yields of CNM-750, CNM-850 and CNM-950 are 92.1 wt%, 87.4 wt% and 27.9 wt%, respectively. Although the carbon yields decreased greatly at a high growth temperature of 950 °C due to agglomeration of some metal particles in the catalyst, Fe-Co/CaO catalyst was generally active to the synthesis of CNMs.

We further characterized the structures of as-synthesized CNMs and purified CNMs by XRD. As shown in Fig. 4a, the three CNMs grown on Fe-Co/CaO catalyst at different temperatures all demonstrated strong diffraction peaks from CaO and obvious diffraction peaks at around 26° and 43° corresponding to the (002) and (101) planes of graphitic carbon [25]. However, based on the XRD profiles of the purified CNMs shown in Fig. 4b, CNMs grown at different growth temperatures have a slight difference in the peak around 26°, and the interlayer spacings (d002) of CNMs were calculated, which are listed in Table S1. The d002 of all CNMs are very close to that of highly oriented graphitic carbon (0.34 nm) [45]. The slight difference in the value of d002 may be attributed to the formation of CNMs with different structures [46]. No diffraction peaks of CaO were observed in the purified CNMs, indicating that the HCl treatment can remove the catalyst support. However, the diffraction peaks of FeCo alloy were still observed after purification. Those metal particles may be encapsulated in CNMs, which makes them difficult to be removed completely by dilute acid. As most of metal particles have been removed, the purity of purified CNMs was determined by TGA (Fig. S4). The purity of CNM-750, CNM-850 and CNM-950 after HCl treatment was 96.98 wt%, 95.02 wt% and 97.86 wt%, respectively, which indicated that high-purity CNMs (>95 wt%) were obtained and only a small amount of metal particles (< 5 wt%) remained in those samples.

The microstructure and morphology of the purified CNMs were further examined by SEM and TEM. Fig. 5a, b and c show the SEM images of CNMs grown at 750, 850 and 950 °C, respectively. It can be observed that the structure and morphology of CNMs grown at different growth temperatures are significantly different. CNM-750 contained a large amount of round-like flakes with a broad diameter range of 100–300 nm and a few filamentous carbon with the diameter ranged from 10 to 150 nm. As shown in TEM images in Fig. 5d and Fig. S5a-c, the filamentous carbon are CNFs with a larger diameter (30–100 nm) and multi walled CNTs (MWCNTs) with a smaller diameter (10–20 nm), which were grown from metal particles. Those metal particles were wrapped at the ends of CNTs or CNFs, thus they are difficult to be removed and remained in the purified sample, which are consistent with the XRD and TGA results. Those round-like flakes are GNSs with transparent and smooth surfaces as well as wrinkled edges, which may be composed of a single layer or several graphene layers. From the HRTEM image of GNSs in Fig. 5g, the lattice spacing between lattice fringes of GNSs is about 0.34 nm, which corresponds to the interplanar spacing of the graphite (022) plane [45]. Thus, CNM-750 is a composites of CNTs, CNFs and GNSs (i.e. CNF-GNS-CNT). CNM-850 is CQD-CNT composites as discussed in Section 3.2. The TEM image of CNM-850 (Fig. 5e) also showed that a large amount of CQDs with a size of ~3 nm were attached on the surface of MWCNTs. The HRTEM image of CQDs in Fig. 5h demonstrated that the CQDs have a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, which is assigned to (111) lattice fringes of graphitic carbon [47]. It should be noted that many scattered CQDs with a uniform size of 2–3 nm were also observed by TEM as shown in Fig. 5e and Fig. S5d, which were detached from CNTs due to strong sonication during TEM sample preparation. In contrast, both SEM and TEM images demonstrated that CNM-950 mainly consisted of MWCNTs with a diameter range of 20–55 nm, and the outer wall of MWCNTs was not smooth and a lot of defects were present (Fig. 5f and i). CNFs, GNSs, and CQDs were absent in the CNM-950 sample. But large metal particles of 30–50 nm wrapped by graphitic carbon were observed in CNM-950 as shown in Fig. S5e, and the size of metal particles in CNM-950 was larger than those in CNM-750 and CNM-850, also indicating that some metal particles aggregated at high temperature. It should be noted that CaO was not observed in SEM and TEM images of all the three purified CNM samples, suggesting that the HCl treatment can completely remove CaO, which coincides with the XRD results.

Uv-vis absorption spectroscopy was also used to characterize CNMs grown at different temperatures. As shown in Fig. 6a, neither CNM-750 nor CNM-950 has the characteristic absorption peaks of CQDs, only CNM-850 demonstrated obvious absorption peaks from CQDs, further proving that CQDs only existed in CNM-850, which is consistent with the TEM and SEM results. The Raman spectra of CNMs grown at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 6b. All the three samples have the characteristic Raman peaks (D band and G band). Compared to CNM-850 and CNM-750, CNM-950 has a relatively low D/G ratio, indicating that the CNM-950 has ordered graphite structures with a higher degree of graphitization. In addition, the D band gradually narrowed in the order of CNM750, CNM-850 and CNM-950. Previous studies have found that CQD, CNF and GNS have multiple types of defects, such as vibration modes of sp2-sp3, off-plane defects and functional groups with sp2 bonding [48,49], and usually exhibit a widened D band compared to CNTs [18,50,51]. Thus, Raman results further confirm that different CNMs were produced at different growth temperatures. This is consistent with SEM and TEM results.

Based on the above discussion, it can be concluded that at a low growth temperature of 750 °C, Fe-Co/CaO mainly synthesized CNF-GNS-CNT composites, and with the increase of growth temperature to 850 °C, CQD-CNT composites were produced on the Fe-Co/CaO catalyst, however, when the growth temperature was further increased to 950 °C, only CNTs were grown on the catalyst. Thus, 850 °C is the optimal growth temperature for the synthesis of CQD-CNT composites.

Previous studies have shown that different growth time would also result in the production of CNMs with different structures [52]. Therefore, we further investigate the effects of growth time on the synthesis of CQD-CNT composites from the Fe-Co/CaO catalyst. Except for the growth time of 30 min (i.e. CNM-850), 10 and 60 min were also adopted to synthesize CNMs at the growth temperature of 850 °C, and the as-synthesized samples were labeled as CNM-850-10 and CNM-850-60. Fig. 7a and c show the SEM and TEM images of CNM-850-10, respectively. It can be observed that mainly CNTs (15–30 nm) and CNFs (40–100 nm) are present in the sample synthesized at 10 min, which were grown from metal particles with different sizes (Fig. 7c). Further, the inset in Fig. 7c exhibits that CNFs are composed of stacked graphitic domains structures, which may be attributed to the oxidation attack by OH radicals produced from the decomposition of ethanol [53]. With the growth time increased to 30 min, as discussed in Section 3.3, the synthesized CNM-850 contained only CQD-CNT composites, and CNFs were not observed in the sample. We speculate that CQDs may be formed due to in situ OH-radical oxidation corrosion on those defective CNFs grown at the early stage of CNM growth (i.e. the growth time of 10 min). However, with the further extension of growth time to 60 min, the SEM and TEM images of CNM-850-60 (Fig. 7b and d) both demonstrated that only MWCNTs were synthesized, and neither CQDs nor CNFs were present in the sample. The disappearance of CQDs from the wall of CNTs may be due to their complete oxidation in the subsequent growth (i.e. after a growth time of 30 min). In addition, some small pores with a diameter of 3–5 nm were observed in the wall of the CNTs, as shown by the white arrows in the inset of Fig. 7d, which may also be due to the absent of CQDs or the oxidation of OH radicals.

Fig. S6 shows the Raman spectra of CNMs synthesized at different growth time. We observed that the width of D band also changed significantly with the increase of growth time. In addition, it can be also observed that the intensity of G band decreased with the increase of growth time, indicating that the content of graphitic carbon decreased gradually. We also calculated the carbon yields of CNMs synthesized at different growth time. As listed in Table 1, the carbon yields of CNM-850-10, CNM-850 and CNM-850-60 were 42.9 wt%, 87.4 wt% and 64.8 wt%, respectively, which increased first and then decreased with the increase of growth time. The increase in carbon yield can be attributed to the continuous growth of CNTs, while the decrease in carbon yield after the further extension of growth time was due to the complete oxidation of CNFs and CQDs. The variation of carbon yields and Raman analysis results were consistent with SEM and TEM observation.

Based on the above analysis of CNMs and catalysts, we proposed a mechanism to explain the synthesis of CNTs and CNT-based composites on the Fe-Co/CaO catalyst. First, Fe-Co/CaO was prepared by impregnation of metal acetylactonates on eggshell-derived CaO supports, and the metal species (FexOy, CoxOy) were well dispersed on the catalyst support, as evidenced by the XRD, XPS and EDX results. In addition to Fe-Co/CaO catalysts, growth temperature and growth time were also critical for the synthesis of CNMs via CVD.

Based on the SEM, TEM and Raman results, at a low temperature of 750 °C, mainly CNF-GNS-CNT composites were synthesized on the Fe-Co/CaO catalyst. Previous studies have observed the simultaneous growth of CNTs and CNFs on various catalysts [25,46,54,55]. It is generally believed that the synthesis of CNTs or CNFs is influenced by the size of metal particles in the catalysts, and smaller metal particles tend to grow CNTs, while larger metal particles are favor for the formation of CNFs [46,55]. Based on the TEM images (Fig. S5a-c), it can be also observed that metal particles with a broad size distribution (5–30 nm) were present in the CNM-750 sample, in which small metal particles (~10 nm) were attached at the roots of CNTs, whereas larger metal nanoparticles (~30 nm) were observed at the end of CNFs. In addition, GNSs were also produced in the CNM-750.

Alkali-earth metal oxides such as CaO have been reported to be good catalysts and templates for the growth of GNSs [30]. The synthesis of GNSs is affected by two key factors: sufficient active sites and carbon precursor supply [28]. Thus, alkali-earth metal oxide catalysts with larger specific surface area and pore volume can provide more active sites to adsorb carbon precursors, leading to enhanced quality and yield of GNSs [30]. In addition, the introduction of an auxiliary metal catalyst facilitates the decomposition of carbon precursors, leading to a significant increase in the concentration of carbon species such as CHx, which would also improve the yield and quality of GNSs [20]. In this study, Fe-Co/CaO catalysts were used for the synthesis of CNMs. We infer that the CaO support originated from the decomposition of CaCO3 and Ca(OH)2 during the heating process may act as the catalyst for GNS growth, while FeCo alloy formed during the catalyst reduction played an important role in the decomposition of ethanol and increased the concentration of carbon species. To test this hypothesis, the catalysts were reduced at 700 °C and heated to the growth temperature (750, 850 and 950 °C, respectively) in Ar before cooling down, and the treated catalyst were labeled as 700–750-CAT, 700–850-CAT and 700–950-CAT, which were further characterized by XRD analysis. The XRD results in Fig. S7a demonstrated the diffraction peaks from FeCo alloy and CaO, confirming the formation of FeCo and CaO during the reduction and heating process prior to the CNM growth. We also characterized pore structures of the Fe-Co/CaO catalysts after the reduction and heating process. As shown in Fig. S7b and Table S2, compared to the fresh catalyst, the surface area and pore volume of 700–750-CAT both increased, which may be due to the structural changes caused by the decomposition of CaCO3 to CaO. The increase in surface area and pore volume facilitated the adsorption of carbon precursors and triggered the growth of GNSs.

Based on the above discussion, we proposed that in the presence of metal particles, ethanol molecules would be preferentially adsorbed on the FeCo metal particles, and then decomposed into CHx, which was further dissociated into carbon atoms [56]. The nucleation of carbon atoms on metal particles with different sizes triggered the growth of CNTs or CNFs. However, the relatively low solubility at a low temperature of 750 °C would lead to a rapid saturation of the metal particles, which may cause partial desorption of CHx from the surface of metal particles [57]. The desorbed CHx would be adsorbed by the active sites of CaO, leading to the growth of GNSs. The schematic illustration of CNF-GNS-CNT synthesis on the Fe-Co/CaO catalyst at 750 °C was shown in Fig. 8 .

As the temperature increases to 850 °C, CNMs with different structures were synthesized at different growth time. At an earlier growth stage with a growth time of 10 min, CNF-CNT composites were the main products. As evidenced in TEM images in Fig. 7, metal particles of different sizes in the catalyst lead to the simultaneous generation of CNFs and CNTs, which is similar with the growth mechanism occurred at 750 °C. With the growth time increased to 30 min, as discussed in Section 3.3, the formation of CQDs is due to the oxidation of defective CNFs by the OH radicals generated from the decomposition of ethanol. CNFs were used as a common precursor to synthesize CQDs through chemical oxidation in previous studies, and it is easier to produce GQDs if CNFs already have small graphitic domain structures [50]. It has also been reported that OH radicals have strong oxidation [58], which would easily break those small graphitic domains down into CQDs. In addition, temperature is also a key factor in the oxidation of CNFs, as we did not observe CNFs being oxidized and attacked to produce CQDs at 750 °C. Thus, we believe that the CNF's formation and the subsequent oxidation at a favorable temperature are critical to the synthesis of CQDs. The resulting CQDs with a large π-conjugated network can be well combined with CNTs through π–π stacking to form CQD-CNT composites. With the extension of growth time to 60 min, CQDs would be oxidized by OH radicals, thus, only CNTs were produced in the CNM-850-60 sample. The schematic illustration of CQD-CNT synthesis was shown in Fig. 8. It should be noted that GNSs were not observed in all the samples of CNM-850-10, CNM-850 and CNM-850-60. On the one hand, as shown in Fig. S7b and Table S2, the surface area and pore volume of the catalyst (700–850-CAT) significantly decreased compared to those of 700–750-CAT and fresh catalysts due to the sintering of CaO particles at higher temperatures (> 750 °C), which would be not favorable for GNS growth. On the other hand, the solubility of carbon atoms in metal particles was also enhanced at higher temperatures, and no excessive CHx was desorbed from the metal particles, therefore, GNSs were not produced at 850 °C.

With the growth temperature further increased to 950 °C, CNTs were the main products in the CNM-950 sample. As shown in Fig. S7 and Table S2, the sintering of CaO continues during the temperature ramping to 950 °C, which led to a further decrease in the surface area and pore volume. Meanwhile, the increase in the average size of metal particles at 950 °C compared to those at lower temperatures was also observed in the XRD results (Table S2). Thus, it can be concluded that a large amount of metal particles aggregated to form some large metal particles. These large particles were wrapped by graphite or amorphous carbon generated from the ethanol pyrolysis at 950 °C, causing the partial deactivation of catalysts. Only a few small particles (~15 nm) remained active for CNT growth, leading to a low carbon yield. The schematic illustration of CNT growth at 950 °C was also shown in Fig. 8.

In summary, Fe-Co/CaO and Fe-Co/Nano CaO catalysts were prepared by impregnating eggshell-derived CaO supports with a solution of metal acetylactonates (Fe(acac)3 and Co(acac)2). The catalyst characterization indicated that metal species were well dispersed in both catalysts, and both catalysts were active for the synthesis of CNMs with different structures. Fe-Co/Nano CaO produced high-quality CNTs, while Fe-Co/CaO grew CQD-CNT composites. Fe-Co/CaO was further employed to synthesize CNMs by modulating the growth temperature and growth time. The results show that CNF-GNS-CNT composites were synthesized at a low growth temperature of 750 °C. MWCNTs and CNFs were grown on smaller metal particles (~10 nm) and larger metal particles (~30 nm), respectively, while GNSs were mainly synthesized on CaO, which was affected by the sufficient carbon species and a relatively high specific surface area and pore volume of the catalyst. When the growth temperature increased to 850 °C, CNF-CNT, CQD-CNT, and MWCNTs were grown at different growth time of 10 min, 30 min, and 60 min. The growth of CNF-CNT was due to the size difference in metal particles, while the formation of CQDs was ascribed to the oxidation of CNFs at 850 °C, and the extension of growth time would further oxidize the produced CQDs, resulting in only CNTs remained in the sample. When the growth temperature increased to 950 °C, MWCNTs were the main product grown from small metal particles (<15 nm), and some larger metal particles (> 30 nm) were wrapped by graphite. Overall, this study provided a new approach for the facile synthesis of CQD-CNT composites, which will expectedly expand the usage of CQD-CNT in practical applications.

Hong Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Qiang Chen: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaodong Tang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Xinyue Peng: Validation. Hong Deng: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Open Fund of Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Solid Wastes Pollution Control and Recycling (2020B121201003) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project 51602268).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2021.108657.
